What the HGB model sees for Colorado vs Vegas and Carolina vs Montreal — the four best teams left, and why the underlying numbers don't agree with the bracket results.

The last two Western Conference champions meet tonight in Game 1. Colorado has been the most dominant team in these playoffs — they swept the Kings, beat Minnesota in five, and are scoring at 4.02 G/60 with a 56% xGF% at 5v5. That dominance is real. Vegas has been tested, winning two 4-2 series, but at 5v5 their xGF% sits at 50.6% — basically a coin flip. They've been winning games, not dominating play. Mitch Marner is having the playoff of his career (18 points in 12 games, leading all scorers) and Eichel is right behind him at 15. But the model sees the gap between winning and controlling and gives Colorado a 70% chance to reach the Stanley Cup Final. Vegas has been here before — it's their fifth Conference Final in nine seasons — and history says don't count them out.

![Colorado Avalanche vs Vegas Golden Knights — HGB model prediction](/images/analysis/2026-05-21-conference-finals-preview/col-vgk-prediction.png)

In the East, it's a different kind of series. Carolina is 8-0 and hasn't allowed more than a handful of goals across two rounds. Frederik Andersen is at +14.59 GSAx in 8 games, the highest of any playoff goalie this year by a wide margin. Montreal got here the hard way — back-to-back Game 7 road wins — and Jakub Dobes is at +9.87 GSAx, second only to Andersen. The two best playoff goalies in the field are about to face each other Thursday in Raleigh. Montreal also won all three regular-season meetings against Carolina, outscoring them 15-8. But the underlying numbers tell the real story: Carolina's 5v5 xGF% in the playoffs is 55.47%, Montreal's is 45.88%. The Habs have been winning on margins the play doesn't support, and the model has Carolina at 67%.

![Carolina Hurricanes vs Montreal Canadiens — HGB model prediction](/images/analysis/2026-05-21-conference-finals-preview/car-mtl-prediction.png)

The model likes Colorado in the West and Carolina in the East. Both have the underlying numbers to back it. Both series have a counterfactual that makes the upset credible — Vegas's championship pedigree, Montreal's head-to-head dominance in the regular season. Check back here after each round for updated model numbers and series breakdowns. In the meantime, the [playoff lines data](/stats/lines?game_type=3) and [goalie GSAx leaderboard](/stats/goalies?game_type=3) are live if you want to dig into the numbers yourself.

2026 Stanley Cup Final Preview: Carolina vs Vegas
The model has Carolina at 54.9% — a tighter margin than their dominant underlying numbers suggest. The reason is Carter Hart, and the reason it still leans Carolina is Frederik Andersen.

The model has Carolina at 54.9%. For a team that posted a 58.3% 5v5 xGF% across 13 playoff games, the best of any remaining team by a significant margin, that feels cautious. It should. Vegas is the reason.

The series is tighter than Carolina's underlying numbers suggest, and the gap comes down to two things: Carter Hart running well above expectation, and Jack Eichel being the best player on the ice regardless of what team you root for.

![CAR vs VGK — HGB model prediction](/images/analysis/2026-06-01-stanley-cup-final-preview/car-vgk-prediction.png)

**What Carolina's numbers actually say**

Carolina's process in these playoffs has been dominant. Their 59.8% all-situations xGF% and 60.4% 5v5 CF% are not flukes of schedule or matchup — they swept Ottawa and Philadelphia, then took Montreal 4-1, and controlled play in all three series. The Blake–Ehlers–Stankoven line at 63.4% 5v5 xGF% in 256 minutes together is the most-used forward trio in these playoffs. The Miller–Nystrom pairing at 71.9% 5v5 xGF% in 84 minutes is the best defensive pair in the bracket.

Jaccob Slavin has neutralized every opponent's top center for thirteen games without appearing on a scoresheet — that's his job, it's what makes the 60.4% CF% possible, and it's the kind of contribution that doesn't make a highlight reel. Carolina is built to suffocate.

Frederik Andersen has a +14.59 playoff GSAx in 13 games, saving roughly 1.12 goals above expectation per game. His regular-season GSAx was -1.36 in 35 starts. The sample now has enough games behind it that dismissing it as noise is the less defensible position.

**Why Vegas is actually in this**

Vegas is 12-4 with a 52.6% 5v5 xGF%. That record-to-process gap exists because Carter Hart (+6.22 playoff GSAx, 0.39 goals above expectation per game) has been stopping pucks he shouldn't — running well above his regular-season baseline of -1.49 GSAx. The counterintuitive number: Vegas's 5v5 SF share is 47.9% with a 10.1% shooting percentage. Getting outshot and winning the goal battle has a shelf life.

Eichel is controlling the ice at a level no other player in this Final is matching. He's improving the shot quality of everyone around him at a measurably higher rate than any skater on either side, and 18 points in 16 games is the counting-stat confirmation. Mitch Marner has 21 points. Pavel Dorofeyev — the name most casual fans still don't know entering this series — leads Vegas with 10 goals and has been their most consistent driver of play.

**The goaltending question settles it**

When Andersen (+1.12 goals above expectation per game) meets Hart (+0.39), the goaltending advantage that has kept Vegas alive largely disappears. Carolina's process is cleaner. Their defensive structure is deeper.

Vegas has Eichel and the Final experience. Any individual game is live. But the model sees the goaltending delta and the process delta, and it's pointing at Carolina at 54.9%.

Game 1 is Tuesday, June 2 at Lenovo Center in Raleigh. Playoff [lines data](/stats/lines?game_type=3) and [goalie GSAx leaderboard](/stats/goalies?game_type=3) are live. The [series page](/playoffs/2026) has updated prediction numbers after each game.
